title: Show HN: I was tired of forgotten charges – so I built a subscription tracker
why: Person frustrated with forgotten subscription charges looking for tracking solution. Core pain point Scroll and Find solves (money leak discovery from statements).
reply: The frustration is real—most people find out about forgotten subscriptions by accident or when cash flow tightens. The challenge with manual trackers is they only work if you remember to update them, and they don't catch the subscriptions you never knew existed. We built Scroll and Find to reverse-engineer your actual subscriptions from your bank statement or credit card export (no login, just upload a PDF). It finds every recurring charge, dormant trials still running, and renewal dates you've forgotten. Free to scan; if you want monthly monitoring and alerts, there's a paid plan. Might save you the detective work.

title: Show HN: NumeroMoney – Understand your spending without sharing bank login
why: Real person asking about tracking spending without bank login + discovered money leaks (forgotten subscriptions, duplicate charges). Direct fit for Scroll and Find value prop.
reply: This is exactly the problem we see most—subscriptions pile up silently and duplicate charges slip through because spreadsheets aren't built to catch patterns. The fact you spotted them manually means you're ahead of most households, but it takes time. We built Scroll and Find to do that scanning automatically from bank statements or credit card exports (no login required, just upload). You upload a PDF or CSV, get back a report of every subscription, dormant accounts, and duplicate patterns in 24 hours. Free tier lets you see if we're picking up things you've missed. Worth a shot if you want this to be less manual going forward.

title: Ask HN: General API for accessing bank transactions?
why: Person manually tracking expenses via CSV imports, wants better transaction visibility across multiple accounts. Direct Scroll and Find use case: eliminate CSV hassle, real-time tracking.
reply: The CSV import cycle is painful—you lose visibility mid-month and alignment issues pile up. A few people solve this by connecting to Plaid (despite the business focus) or using aggregate tools like YNAB, but honestly they all want your bank login. If you want to stay private, try uploading statements to a tool that categorizes and spots patterns without touching your credentials—no login needed, just the PDF. (I built one called Scroll and Find that does exactly this—free tier, US-based.)

title: Show HN: Finsight – A Privacy First, AI Credit Card and Bank Statement Analyzer
why: Show HN post about privacy-first bank/credit card statement analyzer with PDF/CSV upload, recurring payment detection, and categorization. This is a direct competitor/adjacent tool; relevant for marke
reply: Worth noting: Finsight is a solid local-first approach. If you're building or looking for statement analysis, the privacy-first model (no login, no cloud) is the right direction. The market's moving away from 'give us your password' tools. Recurring payment detection especially—that's where most people find money leaks they didn't know existed.

title: I built an easier way to track personal spending
why: Person built spending dashboard, explicitly found 'a few hundred dollars in forgotten subscriptions.' Core money-leak discovery use case.
reply: That's exactly the insight most people miss — until they see it categorized and trended, recurring charges are invisible. Finding hundreds in forgotten subscriptions is more common than people think, and usually the first place real savings hide. If you want to help others do the same without building a full dashboard, I built Scroll and Find (free tier) — upload a bank statement PDF, it automatically flags subscriptions, recurring charges, and unusual spending patterns. No bank login, privacy-first. Thought you might find it useful as a funnel into your dashboard for people who aren't ready to self-track yet.

title: Show HN: I collected 1k cancellation URLs and built an iOS app around them
why: Person explicitly states 'Got charged for services I forgot about' and built a subscription tracker. Direct match for forgotten/forgotten subscriptions money-leak problem.
reply: Love that you solved your own problem and then scaled it — that's the best product insight. The fact that companies bury cancellation URLs tells you exactly where the money-leak friction is. If you're ever working with clients or businesses trying to audit their full recurring spend (not just track upcoming renewals, but find what they're actually bleeding month-to-month), there's a free side I built called Scroll and Find that reads spending statements and flags subscription patterns. No bank login, just upload a PDF or CSV. Might be a useful referral layer on top of what you've built.

title: Tell HN: I managed to unsubscribe from Adobe CC without being charged
why: Person tracking subscription costs, discovered hidden charges ($250 early-exit fee), and realized they weren't using the service. Classic money-leak scenario: forgotten/underutilized subscriptions.
reply: That early-termination fee trap is brutal — and you're right, it's intentionally buried in the contract. The monthly-payment structure makes the yearly commitment psychologically invisible until cancellation hits. One thing that helps: before signing up for anything subscription-based, calculate the true exit cost upfront (monthly price × 12 + any termination fee). If you ever want to audit what else might be hiding in your subscriptions or recurring charges, I built a free tool (Scroll and Find) that analyzes spending PDFs — no bank login, just upload your statement. Found a few hundred dollars in forgotten subscriptions for others too.
